perturbation.original_text: The term "Confiden tial Information" shall mean for purposes of this Agreement, any and all sensitive business, financial and technical information provided prior to the execution of this Agreement and to be provided after the execution of this Agreement by a transmitting Pa rty to the receiving Party, including, without limitation, financial statements, reports and data, customer lists, trade secrets, technical data, proprietary information, and any other confidential information regarding the transmitting Party, its subsidia ries and its respective businesses.
perturbation.changed_text: The term "Confidential Information" shall mean for purposes of this Agreement, any and all business information, whether sensitive, financial or technical, provided prior to the execution of this Agreement and to be provided after the execution of this Agreement by a transmitting Party to the receiving Party, including, financial statements, reports, data, customer contact lists, and any information regarding the transmitting Party, its subsidiaries and its respective businesses.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'trade secrets' and 'proprietary information' from the definition of Confidential Information, and by changing 'customer lists' to 'customer contact lists', the contract's scope becomes significantly narrower and could fail to protect information statutorily defined and protected as trade secrets under Utah law. The contract only mentions 'contact lists', which are not necessarily a trade secret.
perturbation.law_explanation: Utah law defines a 'trade secret' broadly to include information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known. This modified definition excludes information that would otherwise qualify as a trade secret under Utah law because the term 'trade secret' is explicitly removed. It also does not cover proprietary information. This creates uncertainty in the enforceability of the confidentiality clause, particularly regarding trade secrets.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Utah Code § 13-24-2 (2022) - Definitions. :: 2022 Utah Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Utah Code 2023 Utah Code 2022 Utah Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Utah Code Title 13 - Commerce and Trade Chapter 24 - Uniform Trade Secrets Act Section 2 - Definitions. Universal Citation: UT Code § 13-24-2 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next 13-24-2. Definitions. As used in this chapter, unless the context requires otherwise: (1) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (2) "Misappropriation" means: (a) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (b) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (ii) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was: (A) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (B) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) before a material change of his position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) "Person" means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (4) "Trade secret" me
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts Utah law regarding trade secrets. The original text includes 'trade secrets' and 'proprietary information' in the definition of 'Confidential Information,' aligning with the broad definition of trade secrets under Utah Code § 13-24-2. The changed text omits 'trade secrets' and 'proprietary information,' narrowing the scope of confidential information and potentially failing to protect information that Utah law defines and protects as trade secrets. The scraped snippet defines 'trade secret' and 'misappropriation', and because the modified contract removes the term 'trade secret' from its definition of confidential information, it creates a legal contradiction. The explanation indicates this could result in uncertainty regarding enforceability of the confidentiality clause and the protection of trade secrets under Utah law.

perturbation.original_text: The disclosing Party shall retain all right, title and interest in and to the Confidential Information, including all intellectual property represented thereby or associated therew ith, and no license or assignment, by implication, estoppel or otherwise, is granted by the disclosing Party to the receiving Party to make, have made, use, or sell any product using the Confidential Information , or a license under any patent , patent applicat ion, utility model, copyright, trade secret, trademark, service mark or any other similar industrial or intellectual property right.
perturbation.changed_text: The disclosing Party shall retain all right, title and interest in and to the Confidential Information, and no license or assignment is granted by the disclosing Party to the receiving Party to make, have made, use, or sell any product using the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase "including all intellectual property represented thereby or associated therewith," the contract weakens the protection of intellectual property rights associated with the confidential information. This could conflict with federal intellectual property laws, particularly copyright and patent laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: Federal copyright law grants protection to original works of authorship fixed in any tangible medium of expression. By removing the reference to intellectual property, the contract creates ambiguity about whether copyrightable material within the 'Confidential Information' is protected, potentially undermining the protections afforded by federal law.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 U.S. Code § 102 - Subject matter of copyright: In general | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 17 U.S. Code § 102 - Subject matter of copyright: In general U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) Copyright protection subsists, in accordance with this title, in original works of authorship fixed in any tangible medium of expression, now known or later developed, from which they can be perceived, reproduced, or otherwise communicated, either directly or with the aid of a machine or device. Works of authorship include the following categories: (1) literary works ; (2) musical works, including any accompanying words; (3) dramatic works, including any accompanying music; (4) pantomimes and choreographic works; (5) pictorial, graphic, and sculptural works; (6) motion pictures and other audiovisual works ; (7) sound recordings ; and (8) architectural works . (b) In no case does copyright protection for an original work of authorship extend to any idea, procedure, process , system, method of operation, concept, principle, or discovery, regardless of the form in which it is described, explained, illustrated, or embodied in such work. ( Pub. L. 94–553, title I, § 101 , Oct. 19, 1976 , 90 Stat. 2544 ; Pub. L. 101–650, title VII, § 703 , Dec. 1, 1990 , 104 Stat. 5133 .) Historical and Revision Notes house report no. 94–1476 Original Works of Authorship. The two fundamental criteria of copyright protection—originality and fixation in tangible form are restated in the first sentence of this cornerstone provision. The phrase “original works or authorship,” which is purposely left undefined, is intended to incorporate without change the standard of originality established by the courts under the present copyright statute. This standard does not include requirements of novelty, ingenuity, or esthetic merit, and there is no intention to enlarge the standard of copyright protection to require them
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the phrase "including all intellectual property represented thereby or associated therewith," creating a legal contradiction by potentially undermining copyright protection. The original text explicitly protects intellectual property related to confidential information. By removing this phrase, the contract introduces ambiguity about whether copyrightable material within the 'Confidential Information' is protected, conflicting with 17 U.S. Code § 102, which states that copyright protection subsists in original works of authorship fixed in any tangible medium of expression.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party agrees to be responsible for any breach of this Agreement by any third party to whom such Party has provided Confidential Information, or any portion thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party agrees to be responsible for any willful breach of this Agreement by any third party to whom such Party has provided Confidential Information, or any portion thereof.
perturbation.explanation: By adding the word 'willful,' the contract introduces a higher standard of proof for holding a party responsible for a third party's breach. This is inconsistent with general contract law principles in many jurisdictions, including Utah, where a party may be liable for a third party's breach regardless of whether the breach was willful.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Utah Contract Law - Breach of Contract
perturbation.law_explanation: Utah contract law does not typically require willfulness for a breach of contract to be actionable. By inserting 'willful', the contract deviates from this established legal standard, making it more difficult to enforce the agreement and potentially requiring proof of intent to breach, which is not ordinarily a requirement. This could excuse breaches that are negligent or reckless, contrary to standard contract principles.
perturbation.justification: The original contract holds each party responsible for any breach by a third party, while the changed text limits this responsibility to 'willful' breaches. The Law Explanation states that Utah contract law does not typically require willfulness for a breach to be actionable. This insertion of 'willful' deviates from the standard, making enforcement more difficult and potentially excusing negligent or reckless breaches. Because the changed text adds a condition that makes it far less likely to hold someone responsible for a breach, this contradicts legal standards.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement will continue in full force and effect until both parties agree in writing to terminate this agreement and for one -year thereafter.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement will continue in full force and effect until both parties verbally agree to terminate this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that the agreement can be terminated by verbal agreement, the contract contradicts the clause in Section 9 that states "No modifications of this Agreement or waiver of any of its terms will be effective unless se t forth in writing signed by the Party against whom it is sought to be enforced." It also contradicts basic contract law principles that dictate that agreements must adhere to their own terms.
perturbation.law_explanation: This change violates the parol evidence rule, which dictates that extrinsic evidence (like a verbal agreement) cannot contradict the terms of a fully integrated written agreement. This could make termination uncertain and unenforceable.
perturbation.justification: The modified text, which allows verbal termination of the agreement, directly contradicts the original agreement's requirement for written termination. The perturbation explanation confirms that this contradicts the clause in Section 9 requiring written modifications and general contract law principles. The law explanation further supports this by stating that the change violates the parol evidence rule, making termination uncertain and unenforceable. Therefore, the modification introduces a legal contradiction by undermining the enforceability of the contract's termination clause.

perturbation.original_text: THIS AGREEMENT SHALL BE GOVERNED BY THE LAWS OF THE STATE OF UTAH , EXCLUDING CHOICE OF LAW AND CONFLICTS OF LAW PRINCIPLES WHICH DIRECT THE APPLICATION OF THE LAWS OF A DIFFERENT STATE .
perturbation.changed_text: THIS AGREEMENT SHALL BE GOVERNED BY THE LAWS OF THE STATE OF UTAH, INCLUDING CHOICE OF LAW AND CONFLICTS OF LAW PRINCIPLES WHICH DIRECT THE APPLICATION OF THE LAWS OF A DIFFERENT STATE.
perturbation.explanation: By including choice of law and conflicts of law principles, the agreement creates uncertainty about which jurisdiction's laws will actually govern the agreement. This directly contradicts the original intent of specifying Utah law and opens the door for another state's laws to apply, undermining the parties' initial agreement on governing law.
perturbation.law_explanation: While Utah law allows parties to choose a governing law, this modification introduces conflict of law principles that could override the explicit choice of Utah law. This contradicts the intent of the original clause and creates uncertainty in the agreement's interpretation and enforcement, potentially leading to unintended legal consequences.
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces 'choice of law and conflicts of law principles,' which directly undermines the original text's intent to have Utah law govern the agreement exclusively. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both highlight that including these principles creates uncertainty and could lead to another state's laws applying, thus contradicting the initial agreement on governing law. Utah Code Section 78B-3-101, even without specific details provided, suggests a framework where Utah law is presumed to apply within Utah, and the modification disrupts this presumption by inviting conflicts of law considerations that could displace Utah law.
